body:
## Summary
The repository claims to support native Android, iOS, and Windows desktop artifacts, but the `apps/mobile` and `apps/desktop` directories do not exist. The release quality gate still checks for these artifacts.

## Evidence
```bash
$ ls apps/
api  web

$ test -d apps/mobile && echo "mobile exists" || echo "mobile missing"
mobile missing

$ test -d apps/desktop && echo "desktop exists" || echo "desktop missing"
desktop missing
```

```js
// scripts/verify-native-artifacts.js
const SEARCH_ROOTS = [
  'artifacts',
  'dist',
  'apps/mobile',
  'apps/desktop/src-tauri/target/release/bundle',
].map((relativePath) => path.join(REPO_ROOT, relativePath));
```

## Impact
- `pnpm verify:native-artifacts` always fails unless artifacts are manually placed in the expected directories.
- The release workflow cannot produce mobile/desktop installers from source.
- `GAPS.md` entries about mobile PWA and native desktop are misleading.

## Suggested fix
1. Either add the missing `apps/mobile` (React Native / Expo) and `apps/desktop` (Tauri / Electron) projects, or remove native artifact requirements from the release gate.
2. Update `GAPS.md` to reflect that mobile/desktop are not yet implemented.
3. If intentionally deferred, document the decision in `docs/adr/`.

## Verification
Run `pnpm verify:native-artifacts` and confirm it passes without manual artifact placement, or update the gate to skip mobile/desktop with documented rationale.
title: [Mobile/Desktop] apps/mobile and apps/desktop projects do not exist

body:
## Summary
The application provides static `privacy` and `terms` pages but has no cookie-consent banner, no GDPR/PDPA data-subject endpoints, and no documented data-retention enforcement beyond log cleanup.

## Missing compliance features
- **Cookie consent**: No banner or consent management for analytics/telemetry cookies.
- **Right to access/portability**: No self-service endpoint for users to export their own data.
- **Right to erasure**: No endpoint for users to delete their account and all associated tenant data.
- **Data retention**: `scheduler.service.ts` cleans old logs, but there is no tenant-wide retention policy for orders, customers, activity logs, or telemetry.
- **Privacy policy**: The page is a short placeholder and does not list retention periods, third-party processors, or user rights.

## Impact
- Launching in EU/EEA or jurisdictions with data-protection laws exposes the project to compliance risk.
- Users cannot exercise basic data-subject rights.
- Telemetry/tracking cookies may be set without consent.

## Suggested fix
1. Add a cookie-consent component that blocks non-essential cookies until accepted.
2. Add user-facing endpoints: `GET /me/export` and `DELETE /me/account` with clear tenant/data ownership rules.
3. Define and enforce retention policies per data category (orders, logs, telemetry) with scheduled cleanup.
4. Expand the privacy page to include retention periods, processors, contact info, and user rights.
5. Add a `docs/compliance/gdpr-roadmap.md` tracking implementation status.

## Verification
Run a GDPR checklist against the app and confirm all required features are implemented or explicitly documented as out of scope.
title: [Compliance] Missing cookie consent, GDPR data-subject rights, and retention policy

body:
## Summary
Out of 73 controllers in `apps/api/src`, 49 do not have `@ApiTags` and many lack `@ApiOperation`, `@ApiResponse`, or `@ApiBearerAuth` annotations. This makes the Swagger/OpenAPI docs incomplete and harder for API consumers to navigate.

## Evidence
```bash
$ find apps/api/src -name "*.controller.ts" | wc -l
73

$ grep -L "@ApiTags" apps/api/src/**/*.controller.ts | wc -l
49

$ for f in apps/api/src/**/*.controller.ts; do
    if ! grep -q "@ApiTags\|@ApiOperation" "$f"; then echo "$f"; fi
  done | head -20
apps/api/src/accounting/accounting.controller.ts
apps/api/src/accounting/chart-of-accounts.controller.ts
apps/api/src/accounting/journal-entries.controller.ts
apps/api/src/ai/ai.controller.ts
apps/api/src/analytics/analytics.controller.ts
apps/api/src/analytics/cashflow.controller.ts
apps/api/src/analytics/churn.controller.ts
apps/api/src/analytics/clv.controller.ts
apps/api/src/analytics/data-contracts.controller.ts
apps/api/src/analytics/forecast.controller.ts
...
```

## Impact
- Swagger UI groups endpoints under "default" for untagged controllers, reducing discoverability.
- Missing response schemas hide error shapes from generated clients.
- Missing `@ApiBearerAuth` on protected endpoints does not document the required `Authorization` header.
- Generated SDKs from OpenAPI are incomplete or misleading.

## Suggested fix
1. Add `@ApiTags('Domain Name')` to every controller.
2. Add `@ApiOperation` summaries to all endpoints.
3. Add `@ApiBearerAuth()` to all endpoints protected by `JwtAuthGuard`.
4. Add `@ApiResponse` for common status codes (400, 401, 403, 404, 429).
5. Add a CI lint rule or audit script that fails when a controller is missing `@ApiTags`.

## Verification
Run `pnpm generate-api-docs` and confirm all controllers appear under named tags in the generated OpenAPI spec.
title: [API Docs] Most controllers lack Swagger @ApiTags and @ApiOperation annotations

body:
## Summary
Several product images in the Next.js web app use `alt=""`, which makes them invisible to screen readers and harms accessibility for visually impaired users.

## Affected code
```tsx
// apps/web/src/app/pos/page.tsx:320
<Image src={resolveProductImageUrl(product.imageUrl)} alt="" ... />

// apps/web/src/app/products/page.tsx:144
<Image src={resolveProductImageUrl(product.imageUrl)} alt="" ... />

// apps/web/src/app/products/[id]/page.tsx:121
<Image src={resolveProductImageUrl(product.imageUrl)} alt="" ... />
```

## Impact
- Screen-reader users cannot understand what the image represents.
- Violates WCAG 2.1 guideline 1.1.1 (Non-text Content).
- Search engines and accessibility audits flag these as violations.

## Suggested fix
1. Use meaningful `alt` text such as `product.name`.
2. If the image is purely decorative, add `aria-hidden="true"` and document why `alt=""` is intentional.
3. Add an ESLint rule (`jsx-a11y/alt-text`) to prevent empty alt attributes.

## Verification
Run an axe-core scan on `/products`, `/products/:id`, and `/pos` and confirm no "Images must have alternate text" violations remain.
title: [Accessibility] Product images use empty alt text

body:
## Summary
`GAPS.md` lists "Mobile PWA manifest and service worker" as completed, but the web app has no `manifest.json` or service worker file in `apps/web/src/app` or `apps/web/public`.

## Evidence
```bash
$ find apps/web -name "manifest.json"
(no results)

$ find apps/web -name "service-worker.*" -o -name "sw.*"
(no results)
```

## Impact
- Users cannot install the app as a PWA on mobile/desktop.
- There is no offline fallback or cached shell.
- The completed gap entry in `GAPS.md` is misleading.

## Suggested fix
1. Add `apps/web/src/app/manifest.ts` or `public/manifest.json` with app metadata, icons, and theme colors.
2. Add a service worker using `next-pwa` or Workbox for caching static assets and API responses.
3. Provide an offline fallback page.
4. Update `GAPS.md` to reflect actual status.

## Verification
Run a Lighthouse PWA audit and confirm the app passes the "Installable" and "PWA Optimized" checks.
title: [PWA] Web app is missing manifest.json and service worker

body:
## Summary
`next.config.mjs` disables ESLint and TypeScript error checking during production builds. This allows type errors and lint violations to slip into deployed artifacts.

## Affected code
```js
// apps/web/next.config.mjs
const nextConfig = {
  eslint: { ignoreDuringBuilds: true },
  typescript: { ignoreBuildErrors: true },
  // ...
};
```

## Impact
- Production builds can ship with TypeScript errors.
- Lint violations (including security-related ones) are not caught at build time.
- CI/type-check gates become less meaningful because the final build ignores the same checks.

## Suggested fix
1. Remove `ignoreDuringBuilds: true` and `ignoreBuildErrors: true`.
2. Fix existing type/lint errors so builds pass.
3. If certain rules must be suppressed, do so via `.eslintrc` or `tsconfig` overrides, not by disabling the entire build gate.

## Verification
Run `pnpm --filter @smart-erp/web build` and confirm the build fails on any ESLint or TypeScript error.
title: [Build] Production build ignores ESLint and TypeScript errors
[API Consistency] Auth endpoints skip the standard {success,data} response envelope
[Monitoring] Global exception filter sends all exceptions (including 400/404) to Sentry

body:
## Summary
Many tables store `productId` (and likely other entity IDs) as plain `uuid` columns without foreign-key references to `products`. This allows orphaned rows and silently breaks referential integrity when products are deleted or merged.

## Affected tables (examples)
- `order_items.productId` — no FK to `products`
- `purchase_orders.productId` — no FK to `products`
- `bills_of_materials.productId` — no FK
- `production_orders.productId` — no FK
- `product_lots.productId` — no FK
- `product_serials.productId` — no FK
- `warehouse_transfers.productId` — no FK
- `scm.productId` — no FK
- `wms.productId` — no FK

## Impact
- Deleting or updating a product does not cascade or block related records.
- Reports and analytics can reference non-existent products.
- Data migrations and deduplication become risky and error-prone.
- E-commerce sync can create order items for products that no longer exist.

## Suggested fix
1. Add foreign-key constraints from all `productId` columns to `products.id` with appropriate `onDelete` behavior (`cascade`, `set null`, or `restrict`).
2. Audit other bare UUID columns (e.g., `customerId`, `supplierId`, `warehouseId`) for missing FKs.
3. Write a data-cleanup migration to delete or fix orphaned rows before adding constraints.
4. Add schema tests that verify every relational column has a foreign key.

## Verification
Run `psql \d <table>` for each table and confirm `FOREIGN KEY` constraints exist on `product_id`.
title: [Data Integrity] Many productId columns lack foreign-key constraints

body:
## Summary
The backup script is a minimal `pg_dump` wrapper with no compression, encryption, retention, or scheduling. It relies on a local filesystem path and is not integrated with CI/CD or off-site storage.

## Affected code
```bash
# scripts/backup-db.sh
pg_dump "$DATABASE_URL" > "$BACKUP_DIR/$FILENAME"
```

## Impact
- Backups are stored as uncompressed SQL, consuming significantly more storage than necessary.
- No encryption at rest; anyone with filesystem access can read the full database contents.
- No retention policy — old backups accumulate until the disk fills up.
- No automated scheduling in production; backups depend on manual execution or external cron.
- No verification that backups are restorable beyond `scripts/verify-backup.js`.

## Suggested fix
1. Use `pg_dump` with custom format and compression (`-Fc -Z9`).
2. Add optional encryption (e.g., `gpg` or age) before storing backups.
3. Implement a retention policy (e.g., keep daily backups for 7 days, weekly for 4 weeks, monthly for 12 months).
4. Add a scheduled GitHub Actions workflow or documented cron job for automated backups.
5. Upload backups to an object store (S3-compatible) with encryption in transit and at rest.
6. Periodically run restore drills and report success/failure metrics.

## Verification
Run `scripts/backup-db.sh` and confirm the output is a compressed, encrypted archive and a restore succeeds.
title: [Backup] Database backup script has no compression, encryption, retention, or scheduling
[Security] Audit logs store request.body without redacting passwords or PII
[Security] Data export returns full PII records inline without redaction or access control

body:
## Summary
The test suite has 2,114 passing tests, but the vast majority are unit tests that deeply mock Drizzle ORM. Real integration tests with a PostgreSQL database and real E2E tests over HTTP are a tiny fraction of the total.

## Evidence
```bash
$ find apps/api/src -name "*.coverage.spec.ts" | wc -l
153

$ find apps/api/src/__tests__ -name "*.spec.ts" | wc -l
78

$ find apps/api/test -name "*.e2e-spec.ts" | wc -l
3

$ grep -l "postgres\|createTestingModule\|INestApplication" apps/api/src/__tests__/*.spec.ts apps/api/test/*.e2e-spec.ts | wc -l
5

$ find apps/api/test -name "*.e2e-spec.ts" -exec grep -l "getHttpServer" {} \; | wc -l
3
```

## Impact
- Coverage metrics are high, but they measure mocked code paths rather than real database behavior.
- Bugs in SQL queries, transaction boundaries, tenant isolation, and race conditions are unlikely to be caught before production.
- Refactoring Drizzle queries is risky because mocks must be updated in lockstep.

## Suggested fix
1. Define a test pyramid target (e.g., 60% unit, 30% integration, 10% E2E) and track it.
2. Convert the most critical coverage specs into integration tests that hit a real Postgres database:
   - Inventory adjustments and reservations
   - Orders and payments
   - Webhook dispatch and idempotency
   - Auth flows and tenant isolation
3. Expand E2E coverage beyond the current 3 files.
4. Rename files that are pure unit tests with mocks from `.integration.spec.ts` to `.unit.spec.ts` to avoid confusion.

## Verification
Run the test command and confirm a CI gate that requires at least N integration/E2E tests per critical module.
title: [Testing] Most tests are heavily mocked unit tests; real integration/E2E coverage is thin
[Rate Limiting] Rate limit state is in-memory and not per-tenant
[Observability] LokiLoggerService is instantiated but never pushes logs to Loki
[Observability] @sentry/node is used but not declared in apps/api dependencies

body:
## Summary
The Next.js web app has no `error.tsx` or `loading.tsx` files in `apps/web/src/app`, despite `GAPS.md` listing these as completed items. Without them, route-level errors and loading states fall back to Next.js defaults, degrading UX.

## Affected code
`apps/web/src/app` — missing `error.tsx` and `loading.tsx` route boundaries.

## Evidence
```bash
$ find apps/web/src/app -name "error.tsx"
(no results)

$ find apps/web/src/app -name "loading.tsx"
(no results)
```

`GAPS.md` states:
- `loading.tsx for all 19 remaining routes` — completed
- `error.tsx for all 21 remaining routes` — completed

## Impact
- Users see the generic Next.js error screen when a route throws.
- No route-level loading skeletons; navigation may feel unresponsive.
- The completed gap entries in `GAPS.md` are misleading.

## Suggested fix
1. Add root-level `apps/web/src/app/error.tsx` and `apps/web/src/app/loading.tsx`.
2. Add per-domain `error.tsx`/`loading.tsx` boundaries for critical routes (dashboard, orders, products, customers, inventory, accounting, reports).
3. Update `GAPS.md` to reflect actual status or remove the misleading completed entries.
4. Add a CI check or audit script that verifies `error.tsx` and `loading.tsx` exist for top-level routes.

## Verification
Run the find commands above and confirm `error.tsx`/`loading.tsx` files exist for the routes listed in the acceptance criteria.
title: [Frontend] Next.js app is missing error.tsx and loading.tsx route boundaries

body:
## Summary
API versioning is configured in `app.module.ts` with `VersioningType.HEADER` and a default version, but no controllers use `@Version()` decorators. This means the versioning feature is effectively dead code and all endpoints implicitly belong to version `1`.

## Affected code
```ts
// apps/api/src/common/api-versioning.ts
export const API_VERSIONING_CONFIG: VersioningOptions = {
  type: VersioningType.HEADER,
  header: API_VERSION_HEADER,
  defaultVersion: API_CURRENT_VERSION,
};
```

```ts
// apps/api/src/app.module.ts
app.enableVersioning(API_VERSIONING_CONFIG);
```

Search for `@Version` or `Version(` across controllers returns no usage.

## Impact
- The `X-API-Version` header is accepted but has no effect on routing.
- Clients cannot rely on version negotiation for breaking changes.
- The `DefaultApiVersionMiddleware` exists solely to inject a header that the framework ultimately ignores.
- Future breaking API changes cannot be introduced under a new version without refactoring all controllers.

## Suggested fix
1. Either remove API versioning configuration/middleware if it is not needed, or annotate controllers with `@Version('1')` and document how clients should send `X-API-Version`.
2. Add a test that asserts a missing or unsupported version header returns 404 when versioning is enforced.
3. Update API documentation to reflect the actual versioning behavior.

## Verification
Run `grep -R "@Version" apps/api/src --include='*.ts'` and confirm controllers are annotated, or remove versioning config if intentionally unused.
title: [Architecture] API versioning is configured but not used by any controller
[Architecture] Feature flag cache is unbounded and can exhaust memory

body:
## Summary
`README.md` does not link to the project's key documentation files in `docs/`, making it hard for users and contributors to discover production setup, security, runbooks, and architecture guides.

## Affected code
`README.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `DEVELOPMENT.md`, `SECURITY.md`

## Evidence
```bash
$ grep -i "docs/" README.md CONTRIBUTING.md DEVELOPMENT.md SECURITY.md
CONTRIBUTING.md:├── docs/             # Technical docs, architecture, runbooks
```

## Impact
- New contributors cannot easily find `docs/production-setup-guide.md`, `docs/security/threat-model.md`, `docs/runbooks/`, or `docs/adr/`.
- Security researchers may miss `SECURITY.md` and the ASVS checklist/threat model.
- Onboarding time increases because documentation is scattered and unlinked.

## Suggested fix
1. Add a "Documentation" section to `README.md` with links to:
   - `docs/production-setup-guide.md`
   - `docs/DEVELOPMENT.md` (or merge with existing `DEVELOPMENT.md`)
   - `docs/security/threat-model.md`
   - `docs/security/asvs-checklist.md`
   - `docs/runbooks/release-playbook.md`
   - `docs/runbooks/rollback-playbook.md`
   - `docs/runbooks/incident-runbook.md`
   - `docs/adr/`
2. Update `CONTRIBUTING.md` to link to `docs/qa/qa-gates.md` and coding standards.
3. Update `SECURITY.md` to link to the threat model and ASVS checklist.

## Verification
Run a link checker or markdown linter to confirm all referenced docs files exist and are reachable from `README.md`.
title: [Docs] README and top-level docs do not link to docs/ guides

body:
## Summary
The release workflow deletes an existing release with the same version before creating a new one. This destroys release history, release notes, and any attached artifacts for that version.

## Affected code
`.github/workflows/release.yml`

```yaml
- name: Create GitHub Release
  env:
    GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
    VERSION: ${{ github.event.inputs.version || github.ref_name }}
  run: |
    gh release delete "$VERSION" --yes 2>/dev/null || true
    gh release create "$VERSION" \
      --title "Smart ERP Next $VERSION" \
      --notes-file release-notes.md \
      --target "$GITHUB_SHA" \
      --draft=false
```

## Impact
- Re-running a release for a patch version wipes the original release notes and artifacts.
- Users who bookmarked or depend on a specific release URL will get a 404 temporarily.
- Docker images with the same tag may already be cached by users, but the release metadata is lost.

## Suggested fix
1. Remove the `gh release delete` step.
2. Use `gh release create ... --generate-notes` or fail fast if the release already exists (`gh release view "$VERSION"` and exit 1).
3. For hot-fix re-releases, use a new patch version (e.g., `v0.3.1`) instead of overwriting `v0.3.0`.

## Verification
Trigger a release for an existing tag and confirm the workflow fails with a clear "release already exists" message instead of deleting history.
title: [CI/CD] Release workflow deletes existing release before recreating it

body:
## Summary
The staging deployment workflow pulls the `:staging` image and starts containers without any health or smoke verification, and provides no rollback mechanism.

## Affected code
`.github/workflows/deploy-staging.yml`

```yaml
- name: Deploy to staging
  uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1.1.0
  with:
    script: |
      docker pull ghcr.io/${{ github.repository_owner }}/smart-erp-next:staging
      cd /opt/smart-erp-next
      docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d --remove-orphans
```

## Impact
- A broken staging image can be deployed unnoticed.
- There is no automatic rollback to the previous healthy image if the new container fails its healthcheck.
- The workflow deploys to staging on every push to `dev`, which may interrupt active testing.

## Suggested fix
1. After `docker compose up`, wait for the healthcheck to pass and run a smoke test (e.g., `GET /health`, login with a staging test user).
2. On smoke-test failure, roll back to the previously running image tag.
3. Use an explicit image tag variable (e.g., `IMAGE_TAG=staging-${{ github.sha }}`) instead of overwriting `:staging` blindly.
4. Consider requiring manual approval for staging deployments or limiting auto-deploy to specific paths.

## Verification
Introduce a broken build on `dev` and confirm the staging deploy fails and automatically rolls back.
title: [CI/CD] Staging deploy has no smoke test or rollback on failure

body:
## Summary
The CI workflow (`ci.yml`) runs lint, type-check, audit, unit tests, DB migrations, API E2E tests, and Playwright E2E tests in a single monolithic job. A failure in any late step invalidates the entire job and makes it hard to identify which subsystem failed.

## Affected code
`.github/workflows/ci.yml` — `test-and-build` job with ~20 steps.

## Impact
- Slow feedback loop: every run must execute the full pipeline even when an early step fails.
- Late-stage failures (e.g., Playwright) waste the time spent on DB setup and API E2E tests.
- Logs are huge and hard to navigate for debugging.
- The Playwright browser cache step uses `actions/cache@v5` but the install step still runs on cache hit due to the `hashFiles('pnpm-lock.yaml')` key not including Playwright version.

## Suggested fix
1. Split the pipeline into focused jobs:
   - `lint-and-typecheck`
   - `unit-tests`
   - `api-e2e`
   - `web-e2e`
   - `container-scan` (already separate)
2. Use `needs` to ensure DB-dependent jobs wait only for the build/setup job.
3. Share `node_modules` and build artifacts between jobs via `actions/upload-artifact` and `actions/download-artifact`.
4. Fix the Playwright cache key to include the Playwright version (e.g., from `package.json` or `pnpm-lock.yaml`).

## Verification
Open a PR and confirm CI reports separate job statuses (e.g., "unit-tests", "api-e2e", "web-e2e") in the PR checks.
title: [CI/CD] CI workflow is a single monolithic job, slowing feedback and wasting resources
[Performance] Universal search uses sequential full-scan ILIKE queries and in-memory ranking
[Performance] Outbox cleanup deletes completed events row by row and never cleans failed events
[Performance] E-commerce sync uses sequential N+1 upserts per product/order

body:
## Summary
A `knip` scan reports 18 unused exports, 19 unused exported types, and several unlisted dependencies/binaries. This dead code increases maintenance burden and confuses new contributors.

## Examples of unused exports
- `ErrorMessages` in `apps/api/src/common/errors/error-codes.ts`
- `Deprecated` decorator in `apps/api/src/common/decorators/deprecated.decorator.ts`
- `getSentryConfig` in `apps/api/src/common/config/sentry-config.ts`
- Multiple DTO/response classes in `apps/api/src/manufacturing/dto/`
- `useSyncStatus` in `apps/web/src/hooks/useSyncStatus.ts`

## Unlisted binaries
- `drizzle-kit` (used in CI)
- `remove-items` (used in `package.json` scripts)

## Impact
- Larger bundle size and longer type-check time than necessary.
- New developers may accidentally consume deprecated or internal types.
- Unlisted dependencies can break in strict package managers or after pnpm changes.

## Suggested fix
1. Remove genuinely unused exports and types.
2. For exports that are intentionally public (e.g., DTOs), either consume them or mark them as `@public` and configure `knip` ignore rules.
3. Add `knip` to CI so dead code does not regress.
4. Move unlisted binaries to `devDependencies` or document them in `packageManager` fields.

## Verification
Run `npx knip --no-exit-code` and confirm zero unused exports remain (or only intentionally ignored ones).
title: [Maintenance] Dead code and unused exports reported by knip

body:
## Summary
`pnpm audit` reports 12 known vulnerabilities in production/development dependencies, including moderate-severity issues in `esbuild`, `postcss`, `uuid`, and multiple DOMPurify XSS bypasses.

## Affected packages (excerpt)
- `esbuild@<=0.24.2` → `>=0.25.0` — development server SSRF/request reflection
- `postcss@<8.5.10` → `>=8.5.10` — XSS via unescaped `</style>`
- `uuid@<11.1.1` → `>=11.1.1` — missing buffer bounds check
- `dompurify@<=3.4.10` → `>=3.4.11` — multiple XSS bypasses (IN_PLACE, hook pollution, shadow root, template expressions)

## Impact
- DOMPurify bypasses can allow stored/ reflected XSS if user-generated HTML is sanitized and rendered.
- PostCSS XSS may affect any feature that transforms or embeds user-provided CSS.
- `esbuild` dev-server issue exposes local development environment to malicious websites.

## Suggested fix
1. Run `pnpm audit --fix` or manually bump the affected packages to patched versions.
2. Verify the CI `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` gate is configured to fail on moderate+ severity for production dependencies, not just high.
3. Add a scheduled dependency audit workflow (e.g., weekly) that opens an issue when new vulnerabilities are found.
4. Review direct usages of `DOMPurify.sanitize` to ensure `IN_PLACE` mode and `RETURN_TRUSTED_TYPE` are not used with attacker-controlled DOM nodes.

## Verification
Run `pnpm audit` and confirm zero moderate/high vulnerabilities remain.
title: [Dependencies] pnpm audit reports 12 known vulnerabilities

body:
## Summary
`getDeliveryLogs` queries `webhookDeliveryLogs` by `subscriptionId` only, without verifying the subscription belongs to the requesting tenant. This allows cross-tenant delivery-log leakage.

## Affected code
```ts
// apps/api/src/webhooks/webhooks.service.ts
async getDeliveryLogs(tenantId: string, subscriptionId: string, limit = 50) {
  return this.drizzle.db
    .select()
    .from(webhookDeliveryLogs)
    .where(eq(webhookDeliveryLogs.webhookId, subscriptionId))
    .orderBy(desc(webhookDeliveryLogs.createdAt))
    .limit(limit);
}
```

## Impact
- A user from tenant A who knows a subscription ID from tenant B can read all webhook delivery logs for that subscription.
- Logs may contain sensitive payload data (order details, customer info, payment events).

## Suggested fix
1. Join `webhookDeliveryLogs` with `webhookSubscriptions` and add `eq(webhookSubscriptions.tenantId, tenantId)` to the where clause.
2. Or, fetch the subscription first and verify `subscription.tenantId === tenantId` before querying logs.
3. Apply the same tenant check to `unsubscribe` and any other subscription-scoped operations if not already present.

## Verification
Create two tenants with webhook subscriptions, then call `GET /webhooks/logs/{tenantB-subscription-id}` as a tenant A user and confirm 403.
title: [Security] Webhook delivery logs can be read across tenants

body:
## Summary
`WebhooksService` accepts arbitrary subscriber URLs and dispatches HTTP requests to them without validation, allowing Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) and internal network probing.

## Affected code
```ts
// apps/api/src/webhooks/webhooks.service.ts
async subscribe(tenantId: string, url: string, events: WebhookEvent[], secret?: string) {
  const sub: NewWebhookSubscription = {
    tenantId,
    name: `Webhook ${new Date().toISOString().slice(0, 10)}`,
    url,
    events,
    secret,
    active: true,
  };
  ...
}

private async deliverWebhook(sub: any, payload: WebhookPayload) {
  const response = await fetch(sub.url, {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: { ... },
    body: JSON.stringify(payload),
  });
  ...
}
```

## Impact
- A tenant can subscribe a webhook URL pointing to `http://localhost:3456/admin` or `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` to access internal services or cloud metadata endpoints.
- Webhook dispatcher can be used as an open proxy to scan the internal network.
- No timeout or redirect limit on `fetch`, so a malicious endpoint can hang the dispatcher or cause excessive redirects.

## Suggested fix
1. Validate URLs with a strict allowlist scheme (`https://` recommended, optionally `http://` for local dev).
2. Block private/reserved IP ranges, localhost, and link-local addresses.
3. Add `fetch` timeout, maximum redirect count, and DNS resolution pinning where possible.
4. Rate-limit webhook deliveries per tenant to prevent abuse.
5. Sign webhooks only when a secret is set; otherwise omit the signature header.

## Verification
Subscribe a webhook to `http://localhost:3456/health` and confirm the API rejects the URL.
title: [Security] Webhook dispatcher allows SSRF via unvalidated subscriber URLs
